data.title: Commodities: Oil Moves Higher As Peace Talks Look Shaky
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy | Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy - Resolution hopes fade

Hopes for a resolution between the US and Iran are fading as peace talks stall. In addition, Iran’s seizure of two vessels attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz suggests disruptions to shipments are set to continue. The market is having to reprice expectations. This saw Brent break convincingly back above $100/bbl. As hopes fade, the reality of the supply disruption will set in, leaving further upside for prices. If no progress is made, the market will become increasingly numb to the noise and headlines that have dictated price action recently.

Meanwhile, we continue to see growing demand destruction in the oil market, a trend that will intensify as Persian Gulf supply disruptions persist. Airlines continue to announce flight cancellations amid a tightening in jet fuel supply and significant price strength. Europe’s jet fuel market is heavily exposed to developments in the Middle East. The region sources the majority of its jet fuel imports from the Persian Gulf. There is a push for Europe to look elsewhere for alternative supplies while also relying heavily on inventory. Jet fuel inventory in the ARA region has been declining rapidly in recent weeks, reaching its lowest levels since the Covid era.

Meanwhile, Energy Information Administration data continue to show that the US is exporting record amounts of oil and refined products, as buyers around the globe seek alternative supplies. Total oil and refined product exports over the last reporting week increased by 137k b/d to 12.88m b/d. The recent increase in exports has been driven predominantly by refined products, with flows rising by 564k b/d week-on-week to break above 8m b/d for the first time. Although the US market has been relatively shielded from Middle East supply disruptions, prolonged instability tightens conditions as global buyers increasingly turn to US supplies.

US commercial crude oil inventories increased by 1.93m barrels over the last week. After taking into account SPR releases, total crude oil inventories fell by 2.21m barrels. Given stronger refined product exports, gasoline and distillate fuel oil stocks fell by 4.57m barrels and 3.43m barrels, respectively. Meanwhile, implied demand for refined products came under pressure over the last week, falling by 1.07m b/d WoW. The move was dominated by a decline in fuel oil and propane demand.

In gas markets, investment funds continued to reduce their net long in TTF over the last reporting week on the back of hopes of a resolution in the Middle East. Funds sold 11.5TWh over the week, leaving them with a net long of 260.2TWh. However, with hopes of a quick resolution fading, this fund selling is likely to ease.

Metals – Cautious optimism, but risks remain

Copper and aluminium edged higher yesterday after US President Donald Trump extended the Iran ceasefire indefinitely, reducing near-term geopolitical risk. However, the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, with Tehran signalling it will not reopen the route while the US blockade persists.

Signals across the metals complex remain mixed. Demand is still under pressure as elevated energy costs weigh on global growth. Aluminium has found support from supply disruptions in the Middle East, which accounts for around 9% of global output. Copper supply faces growing risks amid potential sulphuric acid shortages due to shipping disruptions and China’s planned export suspension from May.

In other base metals, nickel fundamentals are getting tighter. The International Nickel Study Group estimates the market will swing to a 32kt deficit in 2026, from a surplus of 283kt in 2025, reflecting Indonesia’s production quotas and broader fallout from Middle East disruptions. Global output is forecast to fall 4.3% year-on-year to 3.72mt in 2026, while consumption is seen rising to 3.75mt, from 3.6mt this year.

In precious metals, gold and silver rebounded after two sessions of losses, supported by a weaker dollar and easing geopolitical tensions. Gold ETF flows have turned positive over the past three weeks. This points to renewed investor interest following the March sell-off. Holdings rose by 10koz on 21 April, marking a sixth consecutive daily inflow and lifting total holdings to 99.3 moz.

Agriculture – Coffee supported by geopolitical risks and tight inventories

Arabica coffee futures rose sharply yesterday. It gained almost 5% amid persistent Middle East tensions that continue to threaten logistics costs, while exchange‑monitored inventories remain tight. Although the ceasefire has been extended, there is little indication that the Strait of Hormuz will reopen, keeping freight risks elevated. Prices also remain highly exposed to fuel costs, given the long inland transport routes from farms to export ports. ICE‑monitored US port inventories fell for a fifth consecutive session to 518.5k bags as of 21 April. This is reinforcing supply concerns. While Brazil’s upcoming arabica harvest next month may ease availability, near‑term export flows remain constrained by lower stocks and a firmer Brazilian real.

Russia has extended its fertiliser export quota to 20mt for the June–November 2026 period, amid tightening global supply linked to the Iran conflict and shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. The revised cap includes 8.7mt of nitrogen fertilisers, over 4.2mt of ammonium nitrate, and roughly 7mt of complex fertilisers. As the world’s second‑largest fertiliser producer, supplying around 20% of global trade, Russia continues to prioritise domestic needs following an existing 18.7mt quota in place through end‑May.

The effective closure of the Strait has restricted roughly one‑third of global seaborne fertiliser flows, intensifying concerns over food security. While importers seek alternative sources, export restrictions from major producers, including China and Russia, have further tightened availability and driven prices sharply higher. India, the world’s largest urea importer, has been forced to pay significant premiums, agreeing to purchases at prices nearly 90% above its previous tender levels.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sun Life Remains A Bright Spot In A Volatile Market, Despite Elevated Valuation
data.description:
The Thesis: Reaffirm Buy Rating Again

Spring often can bring sunnier skies in my part of the world, and so today I'm following up on a Canadian insurer that has had a bright streak, it seems: Sun Life Financial (SLF), whose other segments also include asset management and wealth solutions.

Although I've covered this stock many times on this platform, I want to point out my last 3 buy ratings since the stock is up since all of them. For instance, it is up around +18% since my bullish coverage in Nov. 2024 and also up since my bullish views in May 2025 and Nov. 2025, proving my thesis, which was driven by factors like strong credit ratings, demand for annuity products, and organic client inflows.

Today's follow-up considers new data and additional angles since my last coverage, summarized in my rating worksheet below:

My updated thesis, even after considering new data and evidence, does not change my prior few buy ratings on this stock, and I remain bullish. There is still compelling strength in terms of macro drivers, organic growth and global brand positioning, strong margins, strong A-level credit ratings, and bullish technical chart patterns, which collectively overwhelm some more neutral signals coming from valuation and price forecasts.

Macroeconomic Factors

To keep this discussion concise, I zeroed in on what kind of macro demand drivers could benefit this sector in the near term, and the niche segments to mention are life insurance, asset management and wealth, and specialized dental/health coverage.

Industry association LIMRA had some positive views on the life insurance space in Sun Life's home market of Canada, for instance, writing on March 17th:

Despite a softening economy, recent carrier investments in technology have improved many aspects of the sales and marketing processes, which LIMRA expects will help propel 2026 toward continued growth.

Further, since Sun Life serves the dental benefits segment in the US, notable on that front is data from research firm Research and Markets, published in another major financial site last November, saying "the U.S. dental insurance market is poised for significant growth, projected to surge from $60.2B in 2024 to $111.89B by 2033, driven by a CAGR of 7.13%."

The above points support an upside view for the specific niche of insurance that Sun Life is in, one very different from property/casualty (P&C), which is typically impacted by catastrophic damage events like weather and fire.

As for the asset management and wealth advisory segments, a macro factor that could impact all firms in this space is rising and falling equity markets, impacting the value of assets being managed and fees earned.

Because of geopolitical events that transpired in the Mideast in March, I don't think we can still count on some of the same market forecasts from December and January necessarily, since the dynamics have changed. However, there seems to still be a lot of confidence in equities. For instance, another financial site reported on Apr. 21st that JPMorgan "raised its year-end price target on the S&P 500 from 7200 to 7600, saying the upgrade was driven entirely by higher earnings."

So, given the combined factors above, I think they overwhelmingly support a highly bullish view in this section.

Competitive Positioning vs. Peers

As I've covered this stock many times, I'm quite familiar with its niche by now, and so for peer comparables, I think 3 good ones are Manulife (MFC), MetLife (MET), and Prudential Financial (PRU). Of these, Manulife is its fellow Canada-based peer. This peer group is in a similar market cap range of $38B - $64B.

Using peer data and momentum data in my worksheet above, I discovered that Sun Life had a strong RoCE of +14.8%, leading this peer group, yet struggled to achieve a positive 5-year revenue CAGR, indicating that it is perhaps stronger in earnings than the top line.

While none of these stocks achieved alpha by beating the 10-year price return of the S&P 500 as a benchmark, they all had positive returns nonetheless, with Sun Life among the better performers at +106% price return, showing there has been lots of investor momentum.

Looking beyond those historic, quantitative metrics, when considering Sun Life on a qualitative basis, I think what strengthens its competitive position is considerable global penetration into the Asia market. For instance, its Q4 FY25 investor deck (pg. 18) showed that Sun Life had considerably high ranks across multiple Asian markets:

We can also see from that same investor deck that Sun Life is no small firm, and with the size and scope it brings to this peer group, I think it can certainly compete in a space dominated by large brands like MetLife and Prudential.

Therefore, I was modestly bullish in this section because the firm clearly can compete on a qualitative basis and in return on equity, as well as share price momentum, but could use some improvement in top line growth.

Company Growth Factors

Next, when it comes to company-specific growth factors, in this business I typically look for evidence of organic growth of insurance customers as well as net inflows of money for the company to manage, and we can get some of this from the FYQ4 results that came out well after my last coverage of this stock.

While income statement data shows us Sun Life saw YoY revenue growth (FY25 vs. FY24), I think that can offer a very limited window into the future, and so more important is recent insurance growth, as it can drive future income from premiums.

From the Q4 investor deck (pg. 3), we can see that the Group insurance segment in Canada can be a key upside driver, as "health & protection sales of $95MM were up 8%, reflecting higher health product sales."

In the US group segment, YoY revenue growth was helped by "large case employee benefits sales in Group Benefits and higher Medicaid sales in Dental," which I think complements my earlier comment that there is demand for dental benefits in the US.

On the asset management front, a notable mention from the same investor deck (pg. 5) is organic growth in the Asia market, as "asset management gross flows & wealth sales of $2B were up 8%, driven by higher fixed income and equity fund sales in India."

Looking ahead to the upcoming earnings results in a few weeks on May 6th, what I'll be watching for is continued organic growth trends, and this is why this topic gets full weight in my rating worksheet.

Complementary to the positive data above is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of +6.9%, so this could be an opportunity to reverse that negative revenue CAGR trend I showed earlier.

Given the organic growth in insurance and asset management, along with favorable forward-looking forecasts, I was highly bullish in this section.

Earnings Outlook

Moving on down the income statement, in this section I'll touch briefly on what could impact earnings. Two margins come to mind: the operating margin, which was really strong from the peer data above, as well as the net profit margin, which is also competitive in this peer group at +10.2%.

We can get a closer visualization of the operating margin trends in the chart I pulled below, showing the trend line rising over the last year for Sun Life, a great sign.

This is a business impacted largely by mortality events/life insurance claims in the life insurance sector, and of course, because insurers like this often hold an investment portfolio, they can be impacted by swings in interest income too. However, I think another key impact could come from falling markets, as this could drive fees on asset management lower, potentially hurting profits too.

Despite those risks in mind, the analyst consensus had a positive earnings consensus for FY26 and FY27, and this supports my modestly bullish view but also means that analysts will be watching in a few weeks if these estimates were met or exceeded. So far, data shows the company has beat the last 4 quarterly earnings estimates, so it has a strong track record so far, and this may be adding to the confidence that probabilistically they could do it again.

Balance Sheet Risk Profile

Next, in terms of the balance sheet risk profile, I considered the debt-to-equity, credit ratings, and asset risk exposure of Sun Life to get a sampling of risk rather than diving into every single risk factor that could exist.

When it comes to D/E, Sun Life was at 0.86, somewhere in the middle of this peer group, so not a major outlier, and so appearing to have modest leverage risk in the context of these peers.

As for what credit rating agencies are saying, it seems very favorable, with Sun Life's company website showing most of its subsidiary companies and parent company, Sun Life Financial, having A-level (investment grade) ratings for the most part.

Notable to mention is what Fitch said in its rating note last October, that SLF has a "strong and diversified international business profile, market leadership in core segments, and lower exposure to riskier legacy liabilities than peers."

I think the low leverage profile along with across-the-board strong credit ratings could continue to appeal to more risk-conscious, conservative investors looking for more defensive positions, especially in the latest period of intense market volatility (oil/energy shocks, wars, etc.).

As for asset risk exposure, we can see from the Q4 deck (pg. 28) that SLF has a significant exposure to assets exposed to interest rate risk (debt securities, loans, mortgages), while a much smaller portion of the portfolio is tied to equities or cash only.

Because of this, I think investors should consider that interest rate risk could mean that market values of fixed-income securities could fluctuate, but also that loans and mortgages could be exposed to delinquency or default risk by borrowers.

A mitigating factor is that interest/dividend income is not the primary money maker for Sun Life, as we see in the income statement, which shows, for instance, that in the quarter ending in December, this type of income was only around 16% of total revenue, while income from premiums/annuities drove over 68% of revenue that quarter.

So, after considering all of the factors in this section, I am modestly bullish once again.

Dividends Outlook

In this part, I want to go over peer data on dividends in my worksheet above. I must admit that this is one of my favorite dividend ideas so far and has just the dividend recipe to look for: a yield in line with peers, a payout ratio well below 75%, and a proven 5-year dividend growth CAGR, showing that this firm was able to grow dividends through various market cycles and shocks.

Some of those shocks included pandemics, inflation, interest rate spikes, and tariff wars, to name a few, so it certainly has not been a calm decade for markets.

In addition, looking ahead, if the positive EPS growth consensus comes true, it could further bolster the dividend growth case, and also the fact that SLF has a low leverage risk, so less cash is tied up in debt payments, leaving more to give back to shareholders. I should note that the firm also does this through share buybacks, as the cash flow statement shows, so it is not only growing dividends but also doing share repurchases, which could support the share price.

Overall, I am highly bullish in this section.

Valuation vs. Upside Potential

For the topic of valuation and price forecast, I used comparison data from Seeking Alpha to create the worksheet above and also compared my price forecast to the average Wall St. rating as of Wednesday.

Sun Life appears considerably overvalued to this peer group, both in fwd P/E and trailing P/B. At the same time, my price forecast for Dec. 2026 expects just +9% upside, while the Wall St. forecast actually expects a -4% downside.

I am therefore not bullish when it comes to this section but neutral. While some may say the overvaluation is justified, consider what I showed earlier, which is that both the operating margin and net margin for Sun Life are not the best in this peer group but rather those of its Canadian peer Manulife, who leads the group in those metrics.

I also would not want to pay a 2.3x multiple on this company's book value when its prominent peers are at lower multiples, and this portfolio still exhibits the interest rate risk I mentioned earlier.

Technical Charting Patterns

In this final section, I study the chart below, which tracks the 1-year share price vs. the 50-day SMA and 200-day SMA:

It seems a buy signal (golden cross) formed earlier this winter, the share price teetered for a while, and more recently showed a major bullish spike above the 50-day SMA. With the 50-day SMA pointing upward and the share price above it, I am more inclined to call this a bullish pattern than a neutral one.

Key Risks to My Thesis

Here is the part where I get to challenge my own bullish thesis. In fact, I identified two of them in my worksheet above. Two factors that I did not include in my rating evaluation that could challenge a buy rating would be the regulatory nature of insurance and the fact that insurance rate hikes usually have to be approved, but also the fact that Sun Life is betting heavily on its growth in the Asia market, so any major downturn in that economy could cause future downside.

Interestingly, that means that the global brand penetration, which I mentioned a few sections ago as a competitive edge, could also pose a risk too if those regions underperform or there is a major drop in client inflows.

Conclusion

To conclude today's follow-up on one of the stocks I've been covering regularly over the last few years, I am reiterating my last two buy ratings on Sun Life.

My rating agrees with the latest buy consensus (as of Wednesday) by Wall St. and the quant rating.

The key takeaway I would leave readers with is that the evidence I presented today shows that Sun Life could continue to be both a great dividend idea and at least a modest growth idea if we put more significance on several fundamentals I presented, in spite of valuation being elevated right now.

Research Methodology Used

The author rated this stock holistically across 8 weighted factors that include both fundamental and technical analysis, focusing on actionable insights geared to medium- and longer-term investors seeking both dividend and growth ideas through selective diversification.

data.title: Northrop Grumman: The Nuclear Triad Winner Is Now A Strong Buy
data.description:
Northrop Grumman (NOC) reported its first-quarter earnings, beating analyst estimates on the top and bottom lines, but failed to lift the guidance for the year despite a bullish tone during the call. The stock has now lost 16% since my last report, bringing it close to the trading range where Northrop Grumman was stuck during the second half of 2025. I believe this leaves Northrop Grumman materially undervalued as a provider of important parts of the nuclear triad. In this report, I discuss Northrop Grumman’s Q1 2026 earnings and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

Northrop Grumman Sees Double-Digit Sales Growth

Northrop Grumman sales grew 4% to $9.88 billion, or 5% organically after excluding the sale of its training services business. Segment operating income grew 89% to $1.1 billion as prior-year headwinds of $477 million from the B-21 program did not recur and in-year growth drivers. At the same time, we note that when we add back this charge to last year’s figure, the segment operating income for Northrop Grumman only grew 2.5%. So, the huge jump in profitability is driven by the absence of the B-21 charge.

Aeronautics sales grew 17% to $3.3 billion, driven by the B-21 program, ramp-up of the E-130J TACAMO (Take Charge and Move Out) program, and classified programs. On the B-21, Northrop Grumman benefited from a sale of an airplane that it previously assigned as a company-owned test aircraft. Furthermore, Northrop Grumman has agreed with the US Air Force to increase production capacity, which provides more favorable program economics. This was partially offset by a decrease in the F/A-18 as the company completed its deliveries for the production program. Operating income grew from a $183 million loss to a $305 million profit. Adjusted for the B-21 charge last year, the increase was 3.7%. Against a 17% increase in revenues and lower unfavourable program cost adjustments, it is not impressive.

Defense Systems sales grew 5% and 10% organically to $1.9 billion, driven by higher volumes on the Sentinel and tactical rocket motors and Integrated Battle Command Systems. Operating income grew 3% to $184 million. The increase is somewhat soft, driven by lower favorable catch-up adjustments for program cost estimates and the divestiture of the training services business. What we also see is that programs are ramping up, but it doesn’t yet translate to improving operating leverage. It demonstrates the impact of development phase ramp-up, which comes at a lower margin, on the Sentinel program and higher volumes on other programs needing investments, lowering the margins in the ramp-up phase. Overall, the margins dropped 20 basis points to 9.7%, and while that doesn’t look impressive, I do believe that Northrop Grumman did well if you consider the divestiture and the margin-diluting phase of the Sentinel and missile and ammunition ramp-ups.

Mission Systems sales rose 2% to $2.9 billion on restricted airborne radar volumes and marine systems programs partially offset by lower volumes on the Scalable Agile Beam Radar program and electronic warfare. Nonetheless, operating income jumped 20%, driven by favorable program cost adjustments, higher volumes, and investments made last year. This drove the margins up by 2.2 points to 15.1%.

Space Systems sales declined 3% to $2.5 billion, driven by the wind-down of the Next Generation Interceptor program as Lockheed Martin (LMT) won the contract for that program and higher costs on the GEM 63XL rocket motor due to a launch anomaly earlier this year driving an adjustment on the revenues through the cost-completion method, which does not only require adjustments to profits but also to revenues. This was partially offset through the ramp-up of the Tranche 3 tracking layer program. Operating income declined 17% to $235, driven by the $71 million unfavorable adjustment on the GEM 63XL rocket motor program. Margins dropped from 11% to 9.5%.

Overall, we saw that programs are ramping up, but during the ramp-up phase, margins may be depressed, which impacts segment-level and company-level margins, while the lower-margin Sentinel program is also disproportionately increasing revenues against profits. Furthermore, there were program winddowns and cost overruns. So, overall, we see a good demand environment and strong sales, but the composite margins are not yet reflecting that.

Northrop Grumman Guidance Remains Unchanged

In my prior report, I already marked the guidance for 2026 as one that was not impressive, and since the company confirmed that same guidance, that view still holds. However, we also should be cognizant of the fact that there are ramp-up inefficiencies and development programs ramping up that make a strong margin lift hard to achieve. For 2026, the company is expecting between $43.5 billion and $44 billion in sales compared to nearly $42 billion in sales in 2025. Segment operating income has been guided at $4.85-$5 billion, indicating segment margins of around 11%. EPS has been guided at $27.4-$27.90 compared to $26.34 a year ago. Free cash flow is guided between $3.1 billion and $3.5 billion compared to $3.3 billion in 2025.

Aeronautics revenues are guided at mid-$13 billion with margins of low to mid 9%, while Defense Systems is guided at mid-to-high $8 billion with 10% margins, mission systems at high $12 billion with high 14% margins, and Space Systems sales at $11 billion with 11% margins. I do believe that as a provider of two components of the nuclear triad, namely the B-21 and the Sentinel ICBM, Northrop Grumman is extremely important to the US deterrent capabilities. However, 2026 is shaping up more like a ramp-up year rather than a breakout year, and we may not see a clear breakout at all in future years either in terms of margin expansion.

Northrop Grumman May Not Impress but is Still a Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Northrop Grumman, we note that EBITDA and free cash flow have not changed dramatically, with a cumulative adjustment of -0.1% through 2028. At the same time, the valuation gap between the 3-year median and the peer group has widened substantially. As a result, I am upgrading the stock from buy to strong buy, as I believe that while Northrop Grumman is unlikely to show impressive growth, its valuation currently does not reflect its key positioning in the US nuclear triad and the long-term revenue tails it brings. My base case price target is $711.67, implying 21% upside and 33% upside to $783 in a more bullish setting.

Northrop Grumman is unlikely to show significant expansion in the margins, and I believe margins around 14% is where the company will remain. However, operating cash flow is showing steady growth. Free cash flow will be down this year as capital expenditures increase, but we note that growth comes at a price. The upside has to be unlocked through investments, and that is what we see this year. At the same time, we note that there is an opportunity to increase the yield on cost. That yield on cost expansion is not necessarily impressive, but according to my calculations from a net debt leverage perspective, there is roughly $5.5 billion of space available for Northrop Grumman to repurchase in excess of what is currently modelled. That would allow the company to buy back around 6.5% of its current market capitalization and could boost the dividend payments per share, which then would boost the yield on cost.

Conclusion: Northrop Grumman is a Strong Buy After Recent Downturn

The upgrade for Northrop Grumman does not come from its earnings strength or any increase in estimates, because the first quarter earnings were not necessarily impressive, and in terms of longer-term estimate changes, we note that there was stability in the estimates. The main item that makes it a buy is the fact that the company’s share prices came down substantially while its importance in the nuclear triad and the defense industry as a whole is rising. The share price currently does not reflect that against the company’s 3-year median EV/EBITDA, let alone the peer group valuation.

data.title: Getty Realty: Steady Growth Continues In Q1
data.description:
Shares of Getty Realty (GTY) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 17%, on top of their dividend. After some concerns in early 2025 about tenant quality due to a bankruptcy, solid ongoing results and rent collections have helped to reduce that fear. Plus, shares have received a modest boost after a peer, Whitestone REIT (WSR), agreed to sell itself at a substantial premium, raising the specter of potential M&A interest in GTY, though that is not my base case. I last covered Getty in February, rating the stock a “Buy,” and since then, shares have gained 3%. With updated financials and M&A activity, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, Getty Realty generated $0.63 in funds from operations (“FFO”), which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew over 10% to $58 million. While FFO per share was up 7%, actual FFO was up 15% from a year ago. As a reminder, per-share growth is consistently lower for the company because it issues stock to finance a portion of its acquisitions, leading to an ever-increasing share count, which dilutes EPS growth. As such, acquisitions have a somewhat smaller impact on per-share results than often perceived. Overall, Q1 was a hallmark of what I expect from Getty: steady organic results and accretive property acquisitions.

Getty continues to have absolutely pristine operating metrics. 99.7% of its portfolio is occupied currently, and not a single tenant has missed a rent payment this year. Tenant rent coverage is 2.5x on average; levels above 1.5x are strong for perspective. Because of this, I view GTY’s rental income as among the most secure in the industry, especially given the relatively steady nature of convenience stores, which are fairly immune from e-commerce pressure. These statistics are also consistent with how GTY has managed its business over multiple years, adding to my confidence in its resilience through cycles.

Over the past few years, Getty has diversified its business, but c-stores are still the core of its locations, at 63% of rent. In particular, it has grown its car wash business, and while that faced some concern last year, those issues proved to be temporary, and results have been resilient. Thanks to its acquisitions, GTY has also better diversified tenants so that the top 10% are 60% of its business, down from 82% pre-COVID.

Beyond this, its leases are long-dated, with just 1% maturing this year. The majority of the leases do not mature for over 10 years. This ensures that occupancy will stay high, and the company was also successful in extending $11 million of leases during the quarter. As a reminder, the majority of its rent escalates annually at a fixed amount every year, while a portion of rents are tied to CPI. 99% of rents escalate at an average of 1.8% per year, providing steady organic revenue growth apart from property acquisitions.

On the acquisition front, Getty tends to be very active, and this quarter was no exception. Getty completed $34 million in deals so far this year, at an attractive initial cash yield of 8%. The company has $125 million of investments under contract, and the majority should close by early 2027. With this backlog, it should be able to continue deploying capital at a similar pace in coming quarters.

Now, as noted above, a fellow convenience store REIT, Whitestone, sold itself a few weeks ago, selling to Ares (ARES). There was significant interest, including appetite from Blackstone (BX) and TPG (TPG). As I have discussed in my past write-ups of Getty, I believe the c-store model is very durable, making it an attractive real estate play, which is why I am not surprised there was interest from multiple players. Getty is substantially larger, but with a $2 billion market cap, a deal is certainly not prohibitive. I do not view a deal as likely, as I expect GTY management is focused on growing the business organically and building scale. With shares near a 52-week high, there would be less pressure to sell, unlike Whitestone, which faced an activist shareholder. Still, the potential interest from PE firms may serve to keep a floor under shares.

Even if no deal ever materializes, this is a net positive for the company. As noted above, GTY uses stock to fund acquisitions. The higher its stock goes, the fewer shares it needs to issue, holding all else equal. As such, the rally in shares over the past year will help make M&A a bit more accretive. When Getty purchases properties, it also tries to lock in its equity costs to ensure the deal will be accretive to shareholders. As a result, it has $172 million of unsettled equity that will go to finance deals this year.

Because of this balanced financing approach, Getty maintains a strong balance sheet with 5.1x net debt/EBITDA. It has no maturities before 2028, limiting its rate sensitivity. Last year, when its stock was weak, it relied a bit more on debt to finance deals, which is why this year has been more equity-centric. Pro forma for its unsettled equity issuance, leverage is closer to 4.2x, which opens the door for more debt-funded M&A, which is a bit more accretive in H2. I am comfortable with leverage running at ~5x over the long term. Shares offer a 5.7% dividend yield, and I view this payout as very secure given the stability of its business and soundness of its balance sheet.

Alongside results, management lifted guidance by $0.02 to $2.50-$2.52. As a reminder, Getty’s guidance never includes M&A it has yet to complete. So as it completes deals and they enter the business, it typically can raise guidance over the course of the year, absent a surprising deterioration in the business. This increase was consistent with my own expectations. Back in February, I was targeting $2.52 in FFO this year, reflecting its pipeline of M&A and property developments.

I still view $2.52 as a reasonable central case, though with the strong size of its backlog, we could see FFO beat this by a couple of pennies. Getty is unlikely to ever be a “high-flying” stock like an AI tech stock may be, but it is exactly what I am looking for when it comes to providing secure income and reasonable growth, which is why I see it as an attractive component of portfolios and likely to generate modest outperformance over time. Its core business provides about ~2% ongoing growth from rent escalators. Beyond that, I expect M&A to provide about a ~2% ongoing tailwind to FFO after factoring in dilution. A 4% growth rate combined with a ~5.7% starting yield points to a ~9.7% long-term return.

Technically, this is a little below my 10% buy target, though when making forecasts, it is important to not fall victim to false precision. The future is unknowable, after all. While slightly below the 10% threshold, I remain a “Buy” on GTY given the stability and predictability of its cash flow, which is valuable, especially in uncertain macro environments. Plus, there is a chance, albeit not my base case, that private equity makes a play here. I continue to view GTY as a great stock for income-oriented investors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Swap Lines Imply Some Pressure
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

There are more pressures, but they are only mild

The market discount through to the end of March centred on the freezing of the Strait of Hormuz as critical. Fast-forward to this week, and equity markets impliedly think it's not that critical after all. The oil price, of course, has come under renewed upward pressure, but only to the extent that it's the correct reaction to the ongoing closure of the Strait and the threat from Iran not to negotiate until the US blockade is removed. And bond yields, too, have shown a tendency to edge higher, in tandem with a re-ratchet higher in front-end inflation expectations (albeit mild). We continue to find ourselves in an uncomfortable holding ground as we await the US and Iran to come to some semblance of agreement. And an agreement of sorts remains the most likely outcome. If not, and things turn materially worse (not our base view), everything sells off (risk assets and bonds), apart from energy prices and volatility.

Our models continue to point to a dominant rate cut outcome in the quarters ahead, and that’s not discounting front-end pricing. A worsening in the war narrative presents the biggest risk to this trade, as the higher inflation outcome would make rate cuts more difficult to deliver, at least in the immediate few months. We still think the war is in a wind-down phase, albeit uncomfortable. But even if the Iran war ended today and the Strait reopened instantaneously (still a stretch), we are left with significant price pressure that has yet to filter through. And it’s not just the wider energy price complex; it’s also the likes of fertiliser, which risk adding to food prices in subsequent months down the line.

Big picture, the US 2/10yr curve remains remarkably flat. Especially considering we are (eventually) nearing the tail end of a rate-cutting process. That aside, we identify room for short-tenor rates to fall and for long tenor rates to remain under sticky upward pressure (at least in the coming month or so). If we are correct, and there is an eventual rebuild in the rate-cut narrative in the coming month, that typically coincides with a richening of the 5yr part of the curve (can trade further through an interpolated line between the 2yr and 10yr rates). We should see some relative flattening on the 2/5yr segment, but more relative steepening on the 5/10yr (while the overall 2/10yr curve comes under steepening pressure).

The dashboard of pain has eased considerably

Swap lines could be opened all the same

In the background, most of the pressure that we identified in previous weeks has ratcheted lower (chart above). That includes the USD cross-currency basis premium, which typically would be a signal of funding pressures. Despite this, Treasury Secretary Bessent has confirmed that some Gulf and Asian centres have shown an interest in getting access to dollars through a swap line. This indicates that there is at the very least a need for dollars, and it's likely a preventative measure rather than reflecting a front-and-centre problem. It shows that access to dollars remains an ongoing thing for the global system, no doubt amplified by higher energy prices (denominated in dollars). Basically, these operate like a cross-currency swap, on a fixed exchange rate and an agreed interest rate. And the US will provide these where needed to avoid dollar funding issues.

Thursday's events and market views

From a data perspective, PMIs will be the highlight, with consensus seeing the eurozone composite index dip from 50.7 to 50.1, so just about in growth territory. The UK composite index is expected to nudge below 50, however, to 49.8. From the US, we have weekly jobless claims numbers, which are seen to move slightly higher.

In terms of supply, we have the US auction 5Y TIPS for a total of $26bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Forget The 40x P/E. Focus On The 10-Year Potential (Upgrade)
data.description:
I started my coverage of Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) in 2024 (check it here); in a few months it will be an analysis that is 2 years old. But even though some time has passed, ISRG’s stock did not appreciate much in price; it kept oscillating, and with the current drop, it is now down more than 8%.

But in the meantime, Intuitive Surgical as a company improved, revenue kept growing, margins kept expanding. This is something we can observe in the Q1 that was just reported. Even with this improvement and with the stock drop, the valuation is still not compelling by itself. We are talking about a forward P/E of 40x+, it still seems “overvalued” when looked at in isolation.

However, I already think that at the current price, there is a margin of safety for Intuitive Surgical. There is a premium in the valuation, but it is a premium that the company deserves due to the dominant position and the growth prospects over the next 10 years.

ISRG’s Q1 Earnings

Both Intuitive Surgical’s top line and EPS in Q1 were a beat, and a relevant beat, with surprises of 5% in revenue and 18% in EPS.

More specifically, we are talking about revenue growth in Q1 of 23% YoY, and in my view, it was very healthy growth. The company’s procedures (from Da Vinci and Ion) grew 17%, driven by Ion, which is in a very interesting ramp-up, growing almost 40%.

And recalling a bit of the Intuitive Surgical case, most of the revenue here is recurring. In Q1, $650 million was classified as systems, $433 million as services, and the largest one was the sale of instruments and accessories, with $1.68 billion. This means that if procedures grew 17%, the hospital is not only using the installed base, which proves the value of ISRG’s system, but also, this customer will have to repurchase instruments to use in its system since it is using it more.

Another point that I liked was that the installed base - which grew 12% - grew showing that new systems are in an interesting ramp-up. Of the 431 Da Vinci installed, 232 were Da Vinci 5. In Q1 of 2025, only 147 were installed. So the base is growing with a better installation volume, and it is also growing with a better mix.

Margins also improved. The adjusted gross profit margin reached 67.8% versus 66.4% YoY, while the non-GAAP operating income margin reached something close to 39% (versus ~34% in Q1 of 2025).

Beyond that, the guidance for 2026 is as follows. A very consistent top line growth, and with expenses growing less, that is, it already implies operating leverage gains and a solid enough momentum.

In other words, in Q1 Intuitive Surgical proved that it is in a good hardware upgrade cycle. It is also in a good momentum for the usage of this hardware, since revenue keeps growing more than system sales, as well as being able to capture operating leverage.

Why The Upgrade To Buy (And Valuation)

The estimated CAGR for the surgical robot systems market is 12.4% until 2030, according to Grand View Research. This is already a favorable tailwind for Intuitive Surgical, but I believe the top-line growth can be much higher than that in this period.

Only in the U.S. are there over 6,000 hospitals. You can argue that the installed base is already large, with more than 11,000 Intuitive systems, and that some of these hospitals do not have the infrastructure to install such a system, whether due to lack of expertise, lack of money, or lack of demand. And that is not wrong; it is a valid concern.

But I believe the total addressable market is not 6,000 hospitals in the U.S. If you believe in this trend, that it really is a system that brings advantages to surgery, precision, time, and similar factors, you also believe that the total addressable market is much larger. Let’s think of a mid-sized hospital that today has 1 Da Vinci system. It trained doctors to use the system, hired services from ISRG, and created a relationship with the company and with this trend.

If it saw improvements in indicators, saw value in the product, and saw that there is demand, if the robot has very high utilization, and a full schedule, it will see value in having two Da Vinci systems. With the Ion system, if there is demand in the hospital for oncology/lung biopsy, which is Ion’s focus, it is likely that they will at least consider installing an Ion system.

In the end, this is massive compounding in the financials. Not only because the larger the installed base, the greater the recurring revenue, but because this entire relationship is long-term. Think that a hospital that already has a Da Vinci will not buy a Da Vinci 5 or an Ion system in a week; it will take months to decide, verify demand, check if its doctors will know how to use it, and train them. So think how large Intuitive Surgical’s base can be in 10 years (especially considering that the ceiling is not 1 system per hospital and the ceiling is not only U.S. hospitals; we are talking about a company with strong international potential). Maybe in 2035 the company will have more than 2 mature systems, still with a very strong moat due to technological and regulatory barriers, and still high margins due to efficiency.

Well, and even though the valuation is not exactly appealing, I think it is “good enough” for the compounding thesis here. If ISRG maintains revenue growth of ~15% and an adjusted operating income margin of 38%, we are talking about a P/OI of ~21x in 2030.

Yes, I know it seems overvalued, and it really is (in part). We are talking about operating income, adjusted, and only in 2030, and even then the multiple is above 20x. But we have to remember that the premium here is for relevant reasons. It is for the relationship, for the moat, for the recurrence, but also for the positive surprises that Intuitive Surgical can report over the next 10, 20 years. Think that with each new system developed, there is a strong expansion in the TAM. That is, in 2035, maybe the top-line growth can accelerate even more, eventually reaching $40, or $50 billion recurring. Of course this will take a long time, but it is possible over decades, and as the market cap is $160 billion, when Intuitive Surgical starts generating $10 billion in free cash flow, the shareholder yield will already be relevant.

Risks for ISRG

But I need to say that even though I agree with this premium valuation, it is still a very delicate point of the case. This is because there are risks and uncertainties, if this trend faces challenges in the coming years, whether due to lack of expertise or due to some macroeconomic/regulatory headwind, this is a big problem for ISRG. There are already more than 11,000 systems installed, and even though there is still a lot of room, if the company does not prove that these systems are valuable even for mid-sized hospitals, we may see some market saturation.

Beyond that, there is also competition. Even if today ISRG is a market leader, several companies are investing to try to compete indirectly or eventually directly with ISRG, such as Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) with Ottava. If this competition intensifies over the next 10 years, expectations for maintaining high gross margins may change (amid a loss of pricing power or a pricing war).

Looking at the valuation again, even though I think it’s “good enough,” these are clearly multiples that already price in a lot of factors. No wonder the valuation rating is a D-. We’re talking about a P/S ratio of over 13x even on a forward basis. That is, the company has to keep growing its top line while maintaining high margins for this valuation to make sense at some point.

Finally, even though ISRG’s financials look very good, I still have some concern with stock-based compensation (SBC). It is not something that makes the company bad or invalidates the case. But the company will have to keep proving that it can reduce this expense relative to revenue as it scales so that the shares outstanding chart in the long term becomes more “attractive”.

Bottom Line

In short, I believe that today ISRG’s case is already valid and can be an investment that will deliver an interesting shareholder yield in the long term, mainly through innovation and compounding. But as I mentioned, there are risks and a relevant premium valuation that needs to be considered. Therefore, I consider ISRG’s stock as a ‘cautious buy’.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Still Room To Run With Silver Near $80
data.description:
Pan American Silver Analysis

Usually, when a stock triples in price, I don't revisit it as a BUY because the valuation has simply become too unattractive for current investors. But Pan American Silver (PAAS) is a different company than the one I wrote up at $16 back in May 2023, and with silver hovering near $80/oz, I think it's worth taking another look. The quick recap: I liked the Yamana Gold acquisition at the time because it gave Pan American four producing mines at what I considered a steal. What a buy that turned out to be.

As of writing three years later, the stock trades around $56 and carries a forward P/E of roughly 12x. That's not dirt cheap, and there are certainly cheaper miners on the market today. But for the single best pure-play producer on the silver price (which I'll back up below), I think there's still value here.

A Bigger, More Diversified Company

The 2023 version of Pan American looked nothing like this one. Today it runs 10 producing mines across North and South America, spread across seven jurisdictions, with no single jurisdiction accounting for more than 25% of revenue. That's a meaningful risk reducer for a space where one bad election or one permit fight can torpedo a year of production.

In addition to the Yamana Gold acquisition, management made a shrewd move by acquiring MAG Silver in 2025 for its 44% joint venture interest in the Juanicipio mine in Mexico (one of the highest-grade, lowest-cost silver operations on the planet). This was done with silver prices at $40, or nearly half current prices, so such an acquisition today would be far more expensive. I was firmly in favor of that deal when it was announced, and I still am. Juanicipio is the kind of asset you rarely see trade hands, and Pan American now owns nearly half of it.

Adding this all together, this is no longer a one-country, silver-heavy story with a few legacy assets. It's a diversified precious metals producer that just happens to own more silver ounces in the ground than anyone else on Earth.

La Colorada: The Real Story Here

Management recently released a revised PEA on the La Colorada expansion in Mexico, and this is where the next leg of value creation comes from.

The plan calls for a new 15,000 tonne-per-day flotation plant to process resources from the La Colorada vein mine along with high-grade portions of the Skarn deposit mineral resources. Here's the deal:

  • Silver production will average over 19 million ounces annually during the peak five years

  • Mine life extends out to 2049 for the vein mine and as far as 2068 when you include the Skarn

  • All-in sustaining costs come in at negative $22.67/oz—yes, negative, thanks to the lead/zinc/copper byproduct credits

  • Using spot metal prices, the Skarn project alone carries a net present value of just over $5 billion

  • Estimated free cash flow up to $988 million annually

Add the Skarn production to what the existing vein mine will still be producing, and you're looking at close to 20 million silver-equivalent ounces annually from one mining complex. That makes La Colorada the largest top-tier silver mine in the world by size... but more importantly, the cost profile is what gets me excited.

These numbers work because management is leveraging existing infrastructure and accessing new high-grade veins. The cheapest ounce of silver you can produce is the one where your mill is already built and paid for!

2026 Guidance and Why It Matters

2026 guidance calls for a ~14% increase in attributable silver production to 25–27 million ounces, plus 700–750 koz of gold. Costs look roughly flat year-over-year at spot metal prices, and that matters a lot.

At silver near $80 and their AISC assumption sitting around $17/oz at the midpoint, every incremental ounce is dropping something like $60 straight to the margin. This is a hugely profitable company before La Colorada even comes online.

Once the Skarn project is contributing, it becomes more profitable still.

Balance Sheet is Built for This

I'm picky about balance sheets in this sector because miners have a bad habit of over-levering right before metal prices roll over. Pan American isn't doing that.

  • $1.3 billion in cash and short-term investments

  • Total debt of $852 million, but none of it matures until December 2027—that first tranche is only $283M at a 4.625% coupon

  • The remaining $500M at a 2.63% coupon doesn't come due until August 2031

  • $750M undrawn revolving credit facility sitting on top of that

Total available liquidity is north of $2 billion against a debt stack that's well-termed and cheap.

The bottom line is that management has levers to fund the La Colorada expansion, keep paying the dividend, buy back stock opportunistically, and make other strong moves, all without tapping the equity markets.

The Silver Price Angle

Look, the stock is not dirt cheap here. It's tripled. But silver bulls should own this stock, and it's worth explaining why I'd argue this over a dozen other producers.

Pan American has the most silver reserves in the world (more than Fresnillo) and is on track to produce 27 million ounces of silver annually. According to slide 24 of the corporate presentation, PAAS has the highest correlation to the silver price of any major producer (over the past year, at least). That's the whole reason to own it. If you think silver goes to $100, you want the stock that will most cleanly capture that move. If silver rolls over, yes, this stock gets hit harder than most. Leverage to silver cuts both ways. I get that.

For investors who want silver exposure (SLV) with less operational risk, a streamer like Wheaton Precious Metals offers a more conventional risk/reward. But for leverage to the metal itself, this is the name.

The Escobal Optionality Play

Here's the upside case I haven't even touched: Escobal. Pan American's mine in Guatemala has been suspended since 2017 over Indigenous consultation issues. Once upon a time this thing produced over 20 million silver ounces annually at very competitive costs and still hosts roughly 264 million silver ounces in reserves and resources, plus gold, lead, and zinc credits.

None of this is in the stock. None of it is in analyst models. If the consultation process finally wraps up and management can get this mine back online, it effectively doubles Pan American's silver production profile overnight. Fingers crossed—and in the meantime, you're not paying for it.

Risks

I wouldn't be doing my job if I didn't flag the obvious ones. Silver is volatile, and if prices crack back into the $50s or lower, the forward P/E multiple doesn't look nearly as attractive.

Mexico is home to most of the NPV in La Colorada, and Mexican mining policy has become increasingly politically complex in recent years. Execution on a 15,000 tpd flotation plant is not trivial, and PEA economics always get re-cut once you move to feasibility. Escobal is a lottery ticket; don't size the trade around it.

Bottom Line

At $56, Pan American Silver isn't the screaming bargain it was at $16. But it's a fundamentally better company today; it throws off real free cash flow at current silver prices, the balance sheet is fortress-grade, La Colorada is about to get materially more valuable, and Escobal is a free call option. If you believe in silver over the next cycle, this is one of the cleanest ways to own that thesis. I'm staying long.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Manhattan Associates' Moat Remains Wide, And Now Is A Time To Buy
data.description:
Manhattan Associates (MANH) is currently in the middle of multiple compression due to an environment of slowed growth, and this provides a rare chance for qualitative investors to scoop up one of the widest-moat, smallest-cap stocks in the game.

The Company

MANH is an American software company specializing in supply chain and commerce platforms, increasingly via cloud-based subscriptions, alongside services and support. They were founded in 1990 and became public in 1998.

Their business segments are split roughly between services (47%), cloud subscriptions (38%), and maintenance (12%), with minimal contribution from licenses and hardware. This shows a multi-year transition they've been in, away from traditional license-driven revenue toward a higher-margin, recurring SaaS model.

Let’s take a look at their return metrics compared to peers:

Company

Revenue 10-Year CAGR

Median 10-Year ROE

Median 10-Year ROIC

EPS 10-Year CAGR

FCF 10-Year CAGR

MANH

8.1%

52.3%

53.7%

13.8%

14.2%

SAP SE (SAP)

6.9%

15.6%

10.8%

6.2%

10.9%

Oracle Corporation (ORCL)

4.2%

28.9%

13.4%

7%

7.2%

Kinaxis Inc. (KXSCF)

18.6%

17.8%

17.2%

13.4%

16.8%

The Descartes Systems Group (DSGX)

13.2%

18.4%

15.2%

13%

15%

SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC)

16.7%

16.1%

18.9%

16%

19.3%

Source

The larger and more complex the operation, the more MANH thrives. They are vital to their biggest customers, but what their software does isn’t even applicable to most companies that aren’t blue chip sized. They don’t give a transparent list of customers, other than sharing that no customer makes up more than 10% of revenue. Some of their biggest customers most likely include Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and NIKE (NKE).

Capital Allocation

Let's see how capital has been allocated the past decade:

Year

2016

2017

2018

2019

2020

2021

2022

2023

2024

2025

Revenue

605

595

559

618

586

664

767

929

1,042

1,081

EBIT

194

189

134

116

114

134

153

210

262

283

FCF

133

158

130

132

138

181

173

241

286

374

CAPEX

7

6

7

15

3

4

7

5

9

15

Repurchases

168

132

149

121

44

120

204

196

286

315

SBC

16

16

20

32

33

43

59

72

93

111

Source

This is a relatively straightforward story of R&D serving as the primary means of reinvestment. The majority of this R&D is categorized as "maintenance R&D." MANH is a great example of a very high-quality/wide-moat company that has a rather limited reinvestment runway. Quality investors can’t have their cake and eat it too. You can’t have a company with out-of-this-world ROIC and correspondingly high return on incremental capital as well.

They’ve never paid a dividend and have made very infrequent small acquisitions. Most years, the majority of FCF goes towards buybacks. The only issue is that the stock has traded at a premium for so long that it simply lessens the benefit of driving up EPS. I’m not faulting management for that, of course. I like that they are consistent with the share reduction strategy.

They’re one of the few software companies that actually offsets their SBC dilution plus reduces total share count every year, and they should get credit for it. I don’t expect them to change their main strategy of not paying dividends and not chasing acquisitions just for growth’s sake.

Risk

There's a general risk that was recently quite prominent of AI putting an end to all SaaS and VMS companies eventually. This is only true on the most simplified and "lowest end" type of software. Even if you’re not a "techie," you should spend a few minutes creating your own app/software using a no-code tool. Just mess around with it, and you’ll see that, while fun to use, it is rather limiting, especially as you increase complexity. MANH’s platform is designed specifically for extreme complexity. The trust factor is still hugely limiting in regard to AI disruption risk.

The most realistic risk on a fundamental level is the slow burn of their biggest peers catching up to them in terms of their core product being better for the customer. This would take a long time to really show up in financial results, and MANH's stickiness plays a huge role here.

One of the biggest risks then ends up being multiple compression risk, which will be covered in the valuation section.

Earnings

MANH isn’t exactly cyclical in the traditional sense, but their customers definitely spend a bit less during economic downturns and vice versa.

Recent geopolitical disruptions show us how fragile global supply chains remain, though I view these as cyclical pressures rather than structural changes to the company’s long-term opportunity.

I do expect current geopolitical events to affect MANH’s revenue to some extent, farther down the line, but not as an immediate result and in a more roundabout way. Rising food and energy prices for the global consumer put pressure on MANH’s customers, so we can definitely not expect an increase in revenue from any improving macro conditions. I’m not expecting revenue to beat for the next couple of quarters.

Valuation

Shares hit an all-time high in late 2024 and are currently down 56% from that point. Let's take a look at the multiples compared to peers:

Company

EV/Sales

EV/EBITDA

EV/FCF

P/B

MANH

11.8

25.1

31.4

26.8

SAP

5.2

16.7

22.5

4.4

ORCL

9.6

22.9

23.1

22.5

DSGX

10.4

29

34.6

6.7

SPSC

12.9

33.4

36.8

10.8

KXSCF

4.5

25.4

22.1

6.5

I’m not a ‘price first’ investor, yet it still stings to think about paying around 10x sales for a matured software company at its slowest rate of growth. Multiples alone don't tell the full story, and in this case their competitors are often bigger and more diversified.

While the DCF is at the core of my valuation, companies that are strong qualitatively often trade at what seems like a permanent premium. Even if this is true, there is still variance from year to year. Currently the stock is reeling from continued multiple compression. The market was clearly too bullish in late 2024, and now the slowed growth is being recognised in the price.

Right now it’s not a screaming buy, but most likely a rare chance to get a lowered premium over historical averages. Quality-focused investors always face a risk of potentially overlooking higher valuation just to get exposure to such a high-quality stock. I don’t think that’s the case here, as I see the stock around fair value, but considering the historical premium, the opportunity may not last long.

Conclusion

While not a huge company, quality-first investors have known about MANH for a while. It’s usually enjoyed a qualitative premium, but multiple compression. The biggest real risk is overpaying for an extremely high-quality yet slower-growing company. This isn’t the case right now, as I see the stock roughly at fair value. It's worth taking advantage of the opportunity right now, as this is a temporary discount on a normally premium-priced stock.

data.title: NASA: This New ETF Owns SpaceX And Could Lift Off
data.description:
Traders are gravitating to space stocks once again. With the SpaceX IPO expected to launch later this year, a lot of money is heading into the sector. And understandably so. SpaceX looks like it will be one of the largest IPOs of all time.

Furthermore, SpaceX is likely to get added to major indexes shortly after its trading debut. That, in turn, should cause more passive funds to move into SpaceX stock and potentially the space industry more broadly. That makes it time to highlight a new space ETF with a SpaceX angle.

NASA: The Bullish Perspective on This New ETF

I want to be clear, from the jump, that valuations for both SpaceX and the broader space sector seem quite elevated at the moment. From a fundamental standpoint, there is significant risk across the subset of space stocks. However, from a structural perspective, I think the Tema Space Innovators ETF (NASA) has some unique attributes that the broader investment community hasn't become fully aware of yet. This explains my bullish view on NASA, at least for the next six months to a year.

The ETF launched earlier this month. Its largest holding is SpaceX, currently sitting at just over 10% of the overall ETF:

The quick pitch for why NASA would make sense is that investors can get access to a chunky SpaceX position at a reasonable price. In fact, NASA has been trading right around the fund's net asset value ("NAV") meaning that every dollar invested in NASA gets an economic interest in about 10 cents of SpaceX and 90 cents of other space stocks without any premium to NAV.

That's a sharp contrast to other trading vehicles, where premiums have blown out to astounding levels at some points in the past. For example, there is Destiny Tech100 (DXYZ), a fund that I profiled previously. This closed-end fund shot up from the single digits to as high as $100 immediately after launch, as traders picked up on the fact that it had exposure to SpaceX. Shares once again spiked in late 2024 but have slid since then:

DXYZ's NAV sits at $19.97 per share as of the last report, so that's served as a limiter on upside given that folks have to pay a big premium to get SpaceX exposure via Destiny Tech100.

Similarly, the Fundrise Innovation Fund (VCX) started trading in March and skyrocketed higher but quickly retreated:

From a 52-week high of $575 per share, VCX stock has already dropped by about 80%. As a fellow Seeking Alpha author warned, it was not a good idea to overpay for Anthropic and OpenAI. As the headline alludes, that innovation fund's AI investments drove that particular recent hype cycle. But VCX also has SpaceX as one of its top 10 holdings. And, more broadly, the collapse in VCX's share price speaks to the risk of buying a fund at a massive premium to NAV in order to get exposure to privately held company shares.

NASA, by contrast, is an ETF and as such tends to follow its underlying NAV very closely. If NASA is trading at $30, when you buy a share, you are getting $30 of actual stock value in the purchase rather than paying a chunky premium to get exposure to these highly desired private companies.

As an ETF, NASA allows for the creation or elimination of fund shares. This is the mechanism that keeps the share price near NAV. As a consequence of this, NASA is unlikely to see a massive squeeze like we saw with DXYZ or VCX. But, for traders that are confident that valuations are going to rally in the space sector ahead of the SpaceX IPO and that SpaceX is going to have a hot opening, NASA seems like an ideal way to express that conviction.

A Counterpoint: Another ETF Exists That Owns SpaceX

A counterpoint to the bullish NASA argument would be that this is not the first ETF to debut that owns SpaceX. Specifically, the ERShares Private-Public Crossover ETF (XOVR) exists, and SpaceX is its top holding, making up about 11% of the fund.

XOVR has rallied 20% over the past year, which certainly isn't bad, but it's not the sort of fireworks seen at DXYZ or VCX. Why was there no "meme" effect there?

In my opinion, Private-Public Crossover is a hard theme to sell to the public. Retail traders probably aren't going to get excited about XOVR; the concept is too hard to pitch succinctly. And, aside from SpaceX, the rest of the top five holdings aren't particularly exciting. Specifically, they are NVIDIA (NVDA), Meta (META), Affirm (AFRM), and Maplebear, aka Instacart (CART). Big tech with a splash of FinTech lending.

A mix of VC-styled investments combined with a random smattering of Nasdaq-listed stocks doesn't seem like a compelling concept to sell to the general public. It's not one that would go viral on social media. I think that explains why XOVR hasn't really captured all that much public interest.

By contrast, NASA, which invests in SpaceX and also a bunch of small pure-play space companies, is a much easier pitch. And, for what it's worth, NASA is an absolutely perfect ticker for the theme. You could hardly imagine a stronger brand or memetic association to attach to a space ETF.

NASA Could Be a Good Piece of a Long/Short Space Trade

And, for those of you that want a hedged or semi-arbitraged position, there's also the possibility of taking a long/short position here to keep SpaceX exposure while reducing the overall weighting to "space" stocks.

What am I talking about?

Take an ETF like the Procure Space ETF (UFO). I want to stop and congratulate the ETF manager on the ticker symbol, as UFO is another inspired choice.

Unfortunately, Procure isn't in the SpaceX trade. And doubly unfortunately, the top holdings list is full of stuffy satellite-related stocks from years gone by. The #3 holding in UFO is SiriusXM (SIRI), for example. When people are imagining glamorous space exposure, I doubt they are envisioning a Spotify (SPOT) competitor.

Similarly, UFO has other top holdings in legacy satellite communications companies, which are fine businesses but have little-to-no direct upside from the more exciting things that firms like SpaceX and Blue Origin are up to.

If the assumption is that SpaceX and the space sector in general are going to attract capital all year heading into the IPO, we'd expect the other most dynamic pure-play space firms to take in funds, while legacy firms like SiriusXM won't benefit to nearly the same degree. To think about it directly, every time folks buy NASA, that puts more money into NASA's top holdings and creates a feedback loop. Meanwhile, the industrial and consumer holdings in traditional space ETFs like UFO and the ARK Space & Defense Innovation ETF (ARKX) should underperform, and then the momentum funds and algos will pile onto that, rotating money out of space underperformers back into the winners.

Result? NASA gets a virtuous cycle where inflows cause its small- and mid-cap space holdings to surge, and then algos and performance-chasers buy the breakouts, pushing NASA higher and triggering more inflows from people that want to ride the trend. This sort of move exhausts at some point (perhaps once the SpaceX IPO occurs, in this specific case). But it could go on for a good while before ending.

Tema Space Innovators ETF Bottom Line

Space stocks are quite volatile right now, as is befitting of a young industry that is still ramping up. Many of the pure-play space firms aren't profitable yet. There will be serious setbacks and corrections along the way as the industry takes off.

I'm not making an argument that there is deep value to be found in NASA or any of the space ETFs right now. This is a growth industry with a wide range of potential outcomes for investors over the next five to ten years.

That said, from a structural basis, I like the setup of an ETF having SpaceX as its top position heading into the upcoming IPO. And, unlike funds like DXYZ or VCX, NASA is trading right around its NAV, meaning that people don't have to pay up unduly to get exposure to the theme. I also like that NASA's portfolio is more specifically honed in on next-generation space stocks rather than owning a wider basket of space-adjacent industrial and consumer stocks. One nitpick worth mentioning is that NASA has a 75 basis point management fee. That's perhaps justifiable given the unique SpaceX exposure, though I'd like to see the fee move toward 50 basis points over time to make this a more competitive option for long-term investors.

Space stocks have already rallied a lot over the past year or two, and there is downside risk given the higher valuations and uneven profitability metrics for many firms in the industry. On balance, however, there are enough positives to make NASA a speculative buy. It seems likely that a ton of capital is going to be directed at this sector in 2026, and NASA stands to be one of the clearest beneficiaries ahead of the SpaceX IPO.
